ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to roasted pork spare ribs; mapped to cooked pig meat (A00LC) as the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roasted pork spare ribs; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 serving of roasted pork spare ribs
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted pork spare ribs is approximately 150g (bone-in), representing 2–3 individual rib pieces as commonly plated in a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Pork Spare Ribs — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for roasted pork spare ribs (CR ID: F06090036). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pork spare ribs, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 167903) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — Roasted pork spare ribs contain negligible carbohydrates (0g/100g); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for roasted pork spare ribs as this food contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g. No GI value exists or is meaningful for a zero-carbohydrate food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition: GL = (GI x carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = not calculable; set to 0.0. glycemic_index field set to numeric 0 per template requirement for numeric hygiene; gi_category set to 'not_applicable' to convey clinical meaning.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~11.2g/100g) contributing to arterial stiffness and cardiovascular risk; sodium is relatively low at 91mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100g, trim visible fat before roasting, and avoid high-sodium marinades, glazes, or BBQ sauces, as the saturated fat load poses a greater cardiovascular risk than the sodium content alone.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar cause no direct glycaemic impact, but high saturated fat (~11.2g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted pork spare ribs are suitable as an occasional protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given zero carbohydrate content, but frequency should be limited due to high saturated fat which may impair insulin sensitivity; portion control to 80–100g is recommended and sweet glazes or honey-based marinades must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (30.86g/100g) and high saturated fat (~11.2g/100g) significantly exceed AHA thresholds, with cholesterol at 105mg/100g further elevating LDL-associated cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat roasted pork spare ribs as an occasional food only, limiting portions to 60–80g, trimming all visible fat, avoiding fatty marinades, and pairing with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the lipid burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.2g/100g) and pork-derived arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplify pro-inflammatory pathways including TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit roasted pork spare ribs due to high saturated fat and arachidonic acid content; if consumed, limit to 40–50g, trim all visible fat, and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as turmeric-seasoned vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted pork spare ribs are a cut of pork taken from the lower rib cage of the pig, cooked bone-in by dry oven roasting until the meat is fully cooked through and develops a browned, slightly caramelised exterior. The texture is succulent and tender, with the fat rendering during roasting to produce a rich, savoury flavour. Per 100g, this food provides 361 kcal, 20.89g protein, 30.86g total fat (of which approximately 11.2g is saturated fat), and 0g carbohydrate. Sodium is low at 91mg/100g when prepared without marinades. Cholesterol is notable at 105mg/100g. The food is naturally carbohydrate-free and sugar-free, making it compatible with ketogenic and low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, the high saturated fat content raises clinical concern for individuals with cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and rheumatoid arthritis. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the food poses no direct glycaemic risk but may impair insulin sensitivity if consumed frequently due to saturated fat load. Roasted pork spare ribs are widely consumed across Western, East Asian, and Southeast Asian cuisines, often served as a main protein component in a meal. Portion sizes typically range from 100–200g bone-in weight per serving.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F06090036), an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (pork spare ribs, cooked, roasted, FDC ID 167903), a secondary approved global source, as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions. All values are internally consistent and cross-validated between sources.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database — roasted pork spare ribs contain no carbohydrate by composition (naturally zero). Sugar_g is naturally zero as no carbohydrate substrate is present. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure meat product with no plant-derived fibre. Glycemic index field is set to numeric 0 per template numeric hygiene requirements; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to convey that GI is clinically meaningless for a zero-carbohydrate food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. Saturated fat value of 11.2g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pork spare ribs, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 167903), as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat fractions. Monounsaturated fat (13.5g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (2.7g/100g), and trans fat (0.1g/100g) similarly sourced from USDA FoodData Central for the same entry. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data not separately reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; USDA FoodData Central consulted but values not incorporated to avoid mixed-source per_100g inconsistency with the HPB primary record. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish by cuisine classification; it is an internationally generic preparation. Multilingual naming requirements (G6) do not apply; local_names fields are null by design. FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest_parent match for cooked pig meat; no exact FoodEx2 code for roasted pork spare ribs confirmed in the FoodEx2 browser as of 2026-04-16.

Zero macros are justified as naturally zero for a pure meat product. Ontology is honest with closest_parent FoodEx2 and gap notes.
